comments:
This paper addresses an important problem: objectively measuring and estimating the pointwise robustness of neural networks to adversarial perturbations. The authors (1) formalize pointwise robustness and introduce two aggregate statistics (adversarial frequency and adversarial severity), (2) show how checking existence of adversarial examples can be encoded as constraints for piecewise-linear networks, (3) propose a convex restriction (searching within the linear region around a seed input) that yields an LP whose solution upper-bounds the true distance to the nearest adversarial example, and (4) give an iterative constraint-addition optimization to speed up solving. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 (LeNet and NiN) compare the proposed ALP method to an L-BFGS baseline and demonstrate that ALP finds substantially more adversarial examples and yields less-biased robustness estimates; fine-tuning with ALP improves robustness while fine-tuning with the baseline can overfit to that baseline’s adversarial examples.

Strengths:
- The robustness metrics (frequency and severity) are clearly motivated and useful as evaluation tools.
- Exploiting the piecewise-linear structure of ReLU networks to derive a convex restriction is a principled and practical idea; encoding the region where activations remain fixed is natural and yields an LP formulation.
- The iterative constraint solving optimization is a practical empirical contribution that speeds up the LP approach.
- The experiments are meaningful: they show that a commonly-used baseline (L-BFGS) underestimates vulnerability relative to ALP, and they demonstrate overfitting-to-attack when fine-tuning with a single attack algorithm.

Weaknesses / limitations:
- The convex restriction is inherently conservative (it searches only within the linear region around x*), so it may miss adversarial examples that cross activation boundaries. While the authors acknowledge the overapproximation (ρ̂ ≥ ρ), more analysis of the tightness of this approximation and conditions when it is effective would strengthen the work.
- The method is specialized to piecewise-linear activations (ReLU-like); broader applicability to other architectures (e.g., sigmoidal or more complex layers) is limited or would require additional work.
- The algorithm targets only the second-most likely label as the adversarial target for efficiency. This heuristic can miss closer adversarial examples for other labels; an analysis or ablation quantifying how often the true nearest adversarial label differs would be helpful.
- Some implementation choices (e.g., the rounding margin α used during fine-tuning) seem heuristic; more discussion of sensitivity to these hyperparameters would be useful.
- Computational cost, while reasonable for MNIST, becomes nontrivial on larger nets (10–15s per CIFAR input); further scalability discussion or runtime/complexity analysis would be appropriate.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide ablations: effect of searching more than the single second-ranked target; effect of expanding the convex restriction beyond the immediate linear region (if feasible); impact of iterative constraint solver vs full LP on solution quality.
- Include more quantitative analysis of the conservativeness of ρ̂ (e.g., lower bounds or comparisons to alternative search strategies) and statistical significance of reported improvements.
- Release code and LP/solver details so that others can reproduce and build on the approach.

Overall, this is a well-motivated, technically sound paper with practical contributions to evaluating and improving neural network robustness. The limitations are acknowledged and reasonable given the focus; addressing some of the suggested ablations and analyses would increase confidence in the generality of the approach.
